summary: Gruber announces his article in the September 2003 issue of Macworld magazine, covering BBEdit 7.0's built-in CVS integration on Mac OS X. The article focuses on the one thing BBEdit doesn't handle — creating and setting up initial source code repositories. He notes that while CVS is notoriously cryptic, BBEdit makes it simple to use. He encourages readers to buy the physical magazine and write to the editors requesting more of his work.
key_points: Gruber published a print article in Macworld covering BBEdit 7.0's CVS integration
BBEdit simplifies CVS, which is notoriously cryptic even by Unix standards, but doesn't handle initial repository setup
The article exists only in print, not online — a notable detail for a web-native writer
Gruber self-promotes with characteristic dry humor, asking readers to request 'more articles from the same clever author'
money_quotes: While CVS is notoriously cryptic even by Unix standards, BBEdit's integration makes it really simple to use.
If you enjoy it, it probably wouldn't hurt if you wrote an email to their editors to let them know you'd like to see more articles from the same clever author.
The screenshot is intended as a Kubrick reference, not a Stephen King one, but you're free to interpret it either way.
key_insight: BBEdit 7.0's CVS integration tamed one of Unix's most cryptic tools for Mac users, and Gruber used his Macworld debut to establish himself as a bridge between Unix power and Mac sensibility.
